content.content:
1	# Equation Layer — Production Architecture (Unified)
2	
3	> **Status**: Production-ready v3.0 — All issues resolved. All future phases documented.
4	> **Stack**: Python 3.11+ · SymPy · SciPy · NumPy · FastAPI · SQLite · Valkey · Docker (Final Phase)
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Table of Contents
9	1. [Problem & Value Proposition](#1-problem--value-proposition)
10	2. [Core Architecture — 7 Layers + 3 Cross-Cutting Systems](#2-core-architecture--7-layers--3-cross-cutting-systems)
11	3. [All Issues Identified & Their Solutions](#3-all-issues-identified--their-solutions)
12	4. [Layer 3 — Fitter + Validator (Library-First Redesign)](#4-layer-3--fitter--validator-library-first-redesign)
13	5. [Layer 5 — Probabilistic Integrator (Tiered)](#5-layer-5--probabilistic-integrator-tiered)
14	6. [The Observation Fabric — Ingest Pipeline](#6-the-observation-fabric--ingest-pipeline)
15	7. [Technology Stack — Production & Future](#7-technology-stack--production--future)
16	8. [Architecture Extensibility Points](#8-architecture-extensibility-points)
17	9. [Data Flow Walkthrough](#9-data-flow-walkthrough)
18	10. [Failure Modes & Fallback Router](#10-failure-modes--fallback-router)
19	11. [Security & Privacy Architecture](#11-security--privacy-architecture)
20	12. [Implementation Roadmap — All Phases](#12-implementation-roadmap--all-phases)
21	13. [Final Phase — Dashboard, Docker, Kubernetes, Auth](#13-final-phase--dashboard-docker-kubernetes-auth)
22	14. [Redis Replacement — Why Valkey](#14-redis-replacement--why-valkey)
23	15. [Summary — What This System Is](#15-summary--what-this-system-is)
24	
25	---
26	
27	## 1. Problem & Value Proposition
28	
29	## 1. Problem & Value Proposition (continued)
30	
31	The Equation Layer is the reasoning infrastructure for AI agents. Where RAG retrieves text chunks, this derives answers from first-principles mathematics. Where LLMs guess plausible-sounding numbers, this computes from fitted equations with quantified uncertainty.
32	
33	### The Root Problem
34	
35	Every AI agent today asks the LLM to do two jobs simultaneously: reason over relationships between variables, AND express that reasoning in language. LLMs are excellent at the second. They are structurally unreliable at the first. This produces four failures:
36	
37	- **Wrong quantitative answers** -- the LLM generates plausible numbers, not derived ones
38	- **No confidence score** -- cannot distinguish 95% certain from 40% certain
39	- **No causal trace** -- nobody can explain why the agent said what it said
40	- **Inconsistency** -- the same question returns different answers
41	
42	### The Core Insight
43	
44	**Store equations instead of facts. Detect correlation first. Differentiate before answering. Integrate with uncertainty. Let the LLM only speak -- never think.**
45	
46	### What It Is Not
47	
48	- **Not a memory layer.** Memory stores what happened. This stores what governs what happens.
49	- **Not RAG.** RAG retrieves text. This retrieves equations and computes.
50	- **Not a knowledge graph.** Graphs store topology (A to B). This stores dynamics (dB/dA, d squared B/dA squared, rho of A and C).
51	- **Not a fine-tuned model.** Fine-tuning bakes knowledge into static opaque weights. This stores knowledge externally -- live, auditable, updateable from every new observation.
52	
53	
54	## 2. Core Architecture — 7 Layers + 3 Cross-Cutting Systems
55	
56	### 2.1 End-to-End Flow (Production v3)
57	
58	`
59	QUERY + CONTEXT
60	    |
61	    v
62	[Domain Router] -> selects domain + ontology + library partition
63	    |
64	    v
65	L1 Epistemic Parser (LLM, extraction-only)
66	    |
67	    v
68	L2 Correlation Engine + Drift Detector
69	    |
70	    v
71	L3 Equation Library + Fitter/Validator
72	    |
73	    v
74	L4 Computational Core (symbolic + numeric)
75	    |
76	    v
77	L5 Probabilistic Integrator (Laplace / PyMC)
78	    |
79	    v
80	L6 Confidence Gate
81	    |
82	    v
83	L7 LLM Formatter (speak-only)
84	    |
85	    v
86	OUTPUT + TRACE + CONFIDENCE
87	`
88	
89	### 2.2 Layer Responsibilities (Strict Separation of Concerns)
90	
91	- **Domain Router (Pre-Layer)**: classifies query, loads correct ontology, schema version, and equation library partition.
92	- **Layer 1 — Epistemic Parser**: LLM extracts variables, values, units, time windows, entity, and assumptions. **No equation proposal.**
93	- **Layer 2 — Correlation + Drift**: validates relationship strength and flags structural change before fitting.
94	- **Layer 3 — Library + Fitter/Validator**: retrieve-first; if absent, fit canonical families; validate via AIC, holdout MAPE, residual tests.
95	- **Layer 4 — Computational Core**: evaluate equation and derivatives (first/second order, cross-partials).
96	- **Layer 5 — Probabilistic Integrator**: parameter uncertainty + model uncertainty (BMA).
97	- **Layer 6 — Confidence Gate**: hard thresholds, no override.
98	- **Layer 7 — Formatter**: LLM converts structured output to prose only.
99	- **Fallback Router (Post-Layer)**: routes all failures into actionable workflows; never silent.
100	
101	### 2.3 Cross-Cutting Systems (Always-On)
102	
103	- **Epistemic Ledger**: append-only audit trail for every extraction, fit, gate decision, and output.
104	- **Observation Ledger**: normalized structured observations (variable, value, timestamp, entity, unit, provenance).
105	- **Equation Versioning**: shadow evaluation + rollback for every refit; no destructive overwrites.
106	
107	
108	## 3. All Issues Identified & Their Solutions
109	
110	| Issue | Severity | Correct Fix |
111	|---|---|---|
112	| LLM proposes equation forms | CRITICAL | LLM extracts variables only. Canonical families are tried systematically. PySR runs as a primary discovery path. |
113	| No pre-defined library | CRITICAL | Library-first design. Retrieve first, fit second. Cross-domain transfer for cold start. |
114	| Missing Domain Router | HIGH | Add before Layer 1; loads ontology + schema + library partition. |
115	| Missing Ingestion Pipeline | HIGH | Integrate Observation Fabric into main architecture. |
116	| Missing Fallback Router | HIGH | Post-gate routing with actionable outcomes; never silent. |
117	| NetworkX / Ledger not placed | MODERATE | Ledger = cross-cutting; NetworkX moved to roadmap. |
118	| Model uncertainty ignored | HIGH | Bayesian model averaging across all valid families. |
119	| No equation versioning | HIGH | Shadow evaluation; activate only if improved; rollback supported. |
120	| Temporal granularity mismatch | MODERATE | Temporal Alignment Engine in ingestion pipeline. |
121	
122	### The Single Most Important Fix
123	
124	**Remove the LLM from equation form selection.**
125	This is architectural, not a tuning issue. The LLM must never propose the functional form.
126	
127	
128	## 4. Layer 3 — Fitter + Validator (Library-First Redesign)
129	
130	### 4.1 Library-First Rule
131	
132	1. Hash variable set + domain tag.
133	2. Query equation library for exact match.
134	3. If found and not drifted, use it and skip fitting.
135	4. If not found, fit canonical families and store only if valid.
136	
137	### 4.2 Canonical Family Fitting (No LLM Forms)
138	
139	Families tried in order with strict validation:
140	
141	- Linear: y = a·x + b
142	- Power: y = a·x^b
143	- Exponential: y = a·exp(b·x)
144	- Logarithmic: y = a·ln(x) + b
145	- Logistic: y = L / (1 + exp(-k·(x - x0)))
146	- Polynomial (degree <= 2): y = a + b·x + c·x²
147	
148	Validation checks (all required):
149	- Adjusted R² >= threshold
150	- Holdout MAPE <= threshold
151	- Residual randomness test
152	- Structural validity vs domain constraints (e.g., sign of elasticity)
153	- Lowest AIC among valid candidates
154	
155	### 4.3 Rejection Policy
156	
157	Canonical family fitting runs, and PySR runs as a **primary** discovery path in parallel. If both fail, return **insufficient structural model**. **Never guess.**
158	
159	### 4.4 Equation Library Schema (Core Fields)
160	
161	`
162	 equation_id, var_hash, domain, form_family, sympy_ast,
163	 parameters_json, stats_json, n_obs, created_at, last_fitted_at,
164	 drift_status, confidence_level, provenance_list
165	`
166	
167	### 4.5 Equation Versioning
168	
169	- Each equation has many versions.
170	- New fits are evaluated in shadow on recent holdout data.
171	- Only activate if it improves holdout MAPE.
172	- Always retain rollback.
173	
174	
175	## 5. Layer 5 — Probabilistic Integrator (Tiered)
176	
177	### Tier 1 — Laplace Approximation (Default)
178	- Fit returns MAP parameters and covariance.
179	- Approximate parameter distribution as multivariate Gaussian.
180	- Sample parameters and evaluate the equation to generate answer distribution.
181	- Output: mean, standard deviation, 95% CI, tail risk.
182	
183	### Tier 2 — Full Bayesian (Optional, Per Equation)
184	- Use PyMC when strong priors exist and n is small.
185	- Run NUTS, verify convergence (R-hat < 1.01, ESS > 400).
186	- Produce posterior predictive distribution.
187	
188	### Model Uncertainty (Required)
189	
190	Even after selecting the winning family, model uncertainty must be included:
191	
192	- Use Bayesian Model Averaging across all passing families.
193	- Weight each family by exp(-AIC/2).
194	- Output distribution reflects both parameter and model uncertainty.
195	
196	
197	## 6. The Observation Fabric — Ingest Pipeline
198	
199	### 6.1 5-Layer Connector Architecture
200	
201	`
202	SOURCE -> ADAPTER -> SCHEMA INTERPRETER -> OBSERVATION FACTORY -> SYNC + QUALITY GATE
203	`
204	
205	- **Source Layer**: CSV, SQL, SaaS APIs, spreadsheets, streaming.
206	- **Adapter Layer**: converts sources to unified record stream.
207	- **Schema Interpreter**: maps columns/fields to ontology variables.
208	- **Observation Factory**: normalizes units, types, timestamps, entity resolution.
209	- **Sync + Quality Gate**: dedupe, range checks, drift detection, quarantine.
210	
211	### 6.2 Schema Interpreter (Ontology + Confidence)
212	
213	- High-confidence mappings auto-accepted.
214	- Medium/low confidence presented for review.
215	- Ontology grows as users confirm new synonyms.
216	
217	### 6.3 Temporal Alignment Engine (Critical Fix)
218	
219	- Aligns daily/weekly/monthly data into a common granularity.
220	- Blocks incompatible frequency fits.
221	- Tags native frequency per observation.
222	
223	### 6.4 Observation Ledger
224	
225	Each observation is stored as:
226	`
227	{ variable, value, unit, timestamp, entity, provenance, quality_score }
228	`
229	
230	### 6.5 Auto-Refit Trigger
231	
232	New observations for a variable set enqueue refit with shadow evaluation and drift checks.
233	
234	
235	## 7. Technology Stack — Production & Future
236	
237	### Day-1 Production Stack
238	
239	| Component | Choice | Reason |
240	|---|---|---|
241	| Symbolic math | SymPy | exact derivatives, composable |
242	| Optimization | SciPy (L-BFGS-B, trust-region) | reliable nonlinear fits |
243	| Probabilistic integration | NumPy + SciPy | Laplace approximation + bootstrap |
244	| Persistence | SQLite + JSONB | zero ops, structured rows |
245	| API | FastAPI | typed, async, robust |
246	| Correlation + drift | NumPy / SciPy stats | fast partial correlations |
247	| Ledger | SQLite append-only | audit trail |
248	| Cache / queue | Valkey | free Redis-compatible |
249	
250	### Rust Acceleration (Production — gRPC)
251	
252	The following subsystems are implemented in **Rust** for performance and reliability, exposed via gRPC or PyO3:
253	
254	- Observation ingestion engine (CSV/Parquet parsing, schema inference, dedupe, quality gate) via gRPC service
255	- Temporal alignment + aggregation for time-series via gRPC service
256	- Fit kernel hotspots (residuals, MAPE/AIC, bootstrap sampling) via gRPC service
257	- High-throughput append-only ledgers (observation + epistemic logs) via gRPC service
258	- Streaming connector runtimes (Kafka/Kinesis/CDC) via gRPC service
259	
260	### Future Adoption (Trigger-Based)
261	
262	| Tool | Enables | Trigger |
263	|---|---|---|
264	| JAX | GPU autodiff, massive equation graphs | >100-parameter or >1000 QPS |
265	| PySR | symbolic discovery | enabled now as a primary discovery path |
266	| PyMC | full Bayesian | strong priors + small n |
267	| DuckDB/Postgres | analytical queries | large multi-tenant analytics |
268	| Vector DB | semantic equation retrieval | >100k equations |
269	| Graph DB | advanced multi-hop | >10k equation nodes |
270	| Numba | hot loop JIT | profiled bottleneck |
271	
272	
273	## 8. Architecture Extensibility Points
274	
275	`
276	Layer 3 -> Alternative Fitter Slot
277	  Default: SciPy least_squares
278	  Advanced: JAX optimizer
279	  Primary: PySR (parallel discovery, enabled now)
280	
281	Layer 5 -> Uncertainty Engine Slot
282	  Default: Laplace + Bootstrap
283	  Advanced: PyMC MCMC
284	  Future: Variational Inference
285	
286	Layer 4 -> Differentiation Backend Slot
287	  Default: SymPy
288	  Advanced: JAX autodiff
289	
290	Persistence Slot
291	  Default: SQLite
292	  Advanced: Postgres
293	  Future: Vector DB
294	`
295	
296	This keeps the core boring, while allowing measured upgrades.
297	
298	## 9. Data Flow Walkthrough
299	
300	**Query**: "If we raise price by 10%, how does revenue change?"
301	
302	**Observations**: 30 rows of (Price, Revenue, CAC, Churn).
303	
304	**Layer 1**: Extract variables + units only.
305	**Layer 2**: Correlation check, partial correlation, drift check.
306	**Layer 3**: Fit canonical families, select lowest AIC, store equation.
307	**Layer 4**: Evaluate equation and derivatives.
308	**Layer 5**: Generate uncertainty distribution with model averaging.
309	**Layer 6**: Confidence gate checks thresholds.
310	**Layer 7**: Formatter produces prose, no reasoning.
311	
312	**Output**: Answer + CI + sensitivity + trace + warnings.
313	
314	## 10. Failure Modes & Fallback Router
315	
316	### Hard Gate Behavior (Non-Configurable)
317	- n < threshold -> insufficient data
318	- R2_adj below threshold -> reject
319	- holdout MAPE above threshold -> reject
320	- drift detected -> reject or warn
321	- conflict detected -> reject or require review
322	
323	### Fallback Router (Never Silent)
324	
325	If the gate fails:
326	- n too small -> return data collection plan (missing observations list)

# Equation Layer — Production Architecture (Unified)

> **Status**: Production-ready v3.0 — All issues resolved. All future phases documented.
> **Stack**: Python 3.11+ · SymPy · SciPy · NumPy · FastAPI · SQLite · Valkey · Docker (Final Phase)

---

## Table of Contents
1. [Problem & Value Proposition](#1-problem--value-proposition)
2. [Core Architecture — 7 Layers + 3 Cross-Cutting Systems](#2-core-architecture--7-layers--3-cross-cutting-systems)
3. [All Issues Identified & Their Solutions](#3-all-issues-identified--their-solutions)
4. [Layer 3 — Fitter + Validator (Library-First Redesign)](#4-layer-3--fitter--validator-library-first-redesign)
5. [Layer 5 — Probabilistic Integrator (Tiered)](#5-layer-5--probabilistic-integrator-tiered)
6. [The Observation Fabric — Ingest Pipeline](#6-the-observation-fabric--ingest-pipeline)
7. [Technology Stack — Production & Future](#7-technology-stack--production--future)
8. [Architecture Extensibility Points](#8-architecture-extensibility-points)
9. [Data Flow Walkthrough](#9-data-flow-walkthrough)
10. [Failure Modes & Fallback Router](#10-failure-modes--fallback-router)
11. [Security & Privacy Architecture](#11-security--privacy-architecture)
12. [Implementation Roadmap — All Phases](#12-implementation-roadmap--all-phases)
13. [Final Phase — Dashboard, Docker, Kubernetes, Auth](#13-final-phase--dashboard-docker-kubernetes-auth)
14. [Redis Replacement — Why Valkey](#14-redis-replacement--why-valkey)
15. [Summary — What This System Is](#15-summary--what-this-system-is)

---

## 1. Problem & Value Proposition

## 1. Problem & Value Proposition (continued)

The Equation Layer is the reasoning infrastructure for AI agents. Where RAG retrieves text chunks, this derives answers from first-principles mathematics. Where LLMs guess plausible-sounding numbers, this computes from fitted equations with quantified uncertainty.

### The Root Problem

Every AI agent today asks the LLM to do two jobs simultaneously: reason over relationships between variables, AND express that reasoning in language. LLMs are excellent at the second. They are structurally unreliable at the first. This produces four failures:

- **Wrong quantitative answers** -- the LLM generates plausible numbers, not derived ones
- **No confidence score** -- cannot distinguish 95% certain from 40% certain
- **No causal trace** -- nobody can explain why the agent said what it said
- **Inconsistency** -- the same question returns different answers

### The Core Insight

**Store equations instead of facts. Detect correlation first. Differentiate before answering. Integrate with uncertainty. Let the LLM only speak -- never think.**

### What It Is Not

- **Not a memory layer.** Memory stores what happened. This stores what governs what happens.
- **Not RAG.** RAG retrieves text. This retrieves equations and computes.
- **Not a knowledge graph.** Graphs store topology (A to B). This stores dynamics (dB/dA, d squared B/dA squared, rho of A and C).
- **Not a fine-tuned model.** Fine-tuning bakes knowledge into static opaque weights. This stores knowledge externally -- live, auditable, updateable from every new observation.


## 2. Core Architecture — 7 Layers + 3 Cross-Cutting Systems

### 2.1 End-to-End Flow (Production v3)

`
QUERY + CONTEXT
    |
    v
[Domain Router] -> selects domain + ontology + library partition
    |
    v
L1 Epistemic Parser (LLM, extraction-only)
    |
    v
L2 Correlation Engine + Drift Detector
    |
    v
L3 Equation Library + Fitter/Validator
    |
    v
L4 Computational Core (symbolic + numeric)
    |
    v
L5 Probabilistic Integrator (Laplace / PyMC)
    |
    v
L6 Confidence Gate
    |
    v
L7 LLM Formatter (speak-only)
    |
    v
OUTPUT + TRACE + CONFIDENCE
`

### 2.2 Layer Responsibilities (Strict Separation of Concerns)

- **Domain Router (Pre-Layer)**: classifies query, loads correct ontology, schema version, and equation library partition.
- **Layer 1 — Epistemic Parser**: LLM extracts variables, values, units, time windows, entity, and assumptions. **No equation proposal.**
- **Layer 2 — Correlation + Drift**: validates relationship strength and flags structural change before fitting.
- **Layer 3 — Library + Fitter/Validator**: retrieve-first; if absent, fit canonical families; validate via AIC, holdout MAPE, residual tests.
- **Layer 4 — Computational Core**: evaluate equation and derivatives (first/second order, cross-partials).
- **Layer 5 — Probabilistic Integrator**: parameter uncertainty + model uncertainty (BMA).
- **Layer 6 — Confidence Gate**: hard thresholds, no override.
- **Layer 7 — Formatter**: LLM converts structured output to prose only.
- **Fallback Router (Post-Layer)**: routes all failures into actionable workflows; never silent.

### 2.3 Cross-Cutting Systems (Always-On)

- **Epistemic Ledger**: append-only audit trail for every extraction, fit, gate decision, and output.
- **Observation Ledger**: normalized structured observations (variable, value, timestamp, entity, unit, provenance).
- **Equation Versioning**: shadow evaluation + rollback for every refit; no destructive overwrites.


## 3. All Issues Identified & Their Solutions

| Issue | Severity | Correct Fix |
|---|---|---|
| LLM proposes equation forms | CRITICAL | LLM extracts variables only. Canonical families are tried systematically. PySR runs as a primary discovery path. |
| No pre-defined library | CRITICAL | Library-first design. Retrieve first, fit second. Cross-domain transfer for cold start. |
| Missing Domain Router | HIGH | Add before Layer 1; loads ontology + schema + library partition. |
| Missing Ingestion Pipeline | HIGH | Integrate Observation Fabric into main architecture. |
| Missing Fallback Router | HIGH | Post-gate routing with actionable outcomes; never silent. |
| NetworkX / Ledger not placed | MODERATE | Ledger = cross-cutting; NetworkX moved to roadmap. |
| Model uncertainty ignored | HIGH | Bayesian model averaging across all valid families. |
| No equation versioning | HIGH | Shadow evaluation; activate only if improved; rollback supported. |
| Temporal granularity mismatch | MODERATE | Temporal Alignment Engine in ingestion pipeline. |

### The Single Most Important Fix

**Remove the LLM from equation form selection.**
This is architectural, not a tuning issue. The LLM must never propose the functional form.


## 4. Layer 3 — Fitter + Validator (Library-First Redesign)

### 4.1 Library-First Rule

1. Hash variable set + domain tag.
2. Query equation library for exact match.
3. If found and not drifted, use it and skip fitting.
4. If not found, fit canonical families and store only if valid.

### 4.2 Canonical Family Fitting (No LLM Forms)

Families tried in order with strict validation:

- Linear: y = a·x + b
- Power: y = a·x^b
- Exponential: y = a·exp(b·x)
- Logarithmic: y = a·ln(x) + b
- Logistic: y = L / (1 + exp(-k·(x - x0)))
- Polynomial (degree <= 2): y = a + b·x + c·x²

Validation checks (all required):
- Adjusted R² >= threshold
- Holdout MAPE <= threshold
- Residual randomness test
- Structural validity vs domain constraints (e.g., sign of elasticity)
- Lowest AIC among valid candidates

### 4.3 Rejection Policy

Canonical family fitting runs, and PySR runs as a **primary** discovery path in parallel. If both fail, return **insufficient structural model**. **Never guess.**

### 4.4 Equation Library Schema (Core Fields)

`
 equation_id, var_hash, domain, form_family, sympy_ast,
 parameters_json, stats_json, n_obs, created_at, last_fitted_at,
 drift_status, confidence_level, provenance_list
`

### 4.5 Equation Versioning

- Each equation has many versions.
- New fits are evaluated in shadow on recent holdout data.
- Only activate if it improves holdout MAPE.
- Always retain rollback.


## 5. Layer 5 — Probabilistic Integrator (Tiered)

### Tier 1 — Laplace Approximation (Default)
- Fit returns MAP parameters and covariance.
- Approximate parameter distribution as multivariate Gaussian.
- Sample parameters and evaluate the equation to generate answer distribution.
- Output: mean, standard deviation, 95% CI, tail risk.

### Tier 2 — Full Bayesian (Optional, Per Equation)
- Use PyMC when strong priors exist and n is small.
- Run NUTS, verify convergence (R-hat < 1.01, ESS > 400).
- Produce posterior predictive distribution.

### Model Uncertainty (Required)

Even after selecting the winning family, model uncertainty must be included:

- Use Bayesian Model Averaging across all passing families.
- Weight each family by exp(-AIC/2).
- Output distribution reflects both parameter and model uncertainty.


## 6. The Observation Fabric — Ingest Pipeline

### 6.1 5-Layer Connector Architecture

`
SOURCE -> ADAPTER -> SCHEMA INTERPRETER -> OBSERVATION FACTORY -> SYNC + QUALITY GATE
`

- **Source Layer**: CSV, SQL, SaaS APIs, spreadsheets, streaming.
- **Adapter Layer**: converts sources to unified record stream.
- **Schema Interpreter**: maps columns/fields to ontology variables.
- **Observation Factory**: normalizes units, types, timestamps, entity resolution.
- **Sync + Quality Gate**: dedupe, range checks, drift detection, quarantine.

### 6.2 Schema Interpreter (Ontology + Confidence)

- High-confidence mappings auto-accepted.
- Medium/low confidence presented for review.
- Ontology grows as users confirm new synonyms.

### 6.3 Temporal Alignment Engine (Critical Fix)

- Aligns daily/weekly/monthly data into a common granularity.
- Blocks incompatible frequency fits.
- Tags native frequency per observation.

### 6.4 Observation Ledger

Each observation is stored as:
`
{ variable, value, unit, timestamp, entity, provenance, quality_score }
`

### 6.5 Auto-Refit Trigger

New observations for a variable set enqueue refit with shadow evaluation and drift checks.


## 7. Technology Stack — Production & Future

### Day-1 Production Stack

| Component | Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Symbolic math | SymPy | exact derivatives, composable |
| Optimization | SciPy (L-BFGS-B, trust-region) | reliable nonlinear fits |
| Probabilistic integration | NumPy + SciPy | Laplace approximation + bootstrap |
| Persistence | SQLite + JSONB | zero ops, structured rows |
| API | FastAPI | typed, async, robust |
| Correlation + drift | NumPy / SciPy stats | fast partial correlations |
| Ledger | SQLite append-only | audit trail |
| Cache / queue | Valkey | free Redis-compatible |

### Rust Acceleration (Production — gRPC)

The following subsystems are implemented in **Rust** for performance and reliability, exposed via gRPC or PyO3:

- Observation ingestion engine (CSV/Parquet parsing, schema inference, dedupe, quality gate) via gRPC service
- Temporal alignment + aggregation for time-series via gRPC service
- Fit kernel hotspots (residuals, MAPE/AIC, bootstrap sampling) via gRPC service
- High-throughput append-only ledgers (observation + epistemic logs) via gRPC service
- Streaming connector runtimes (Kafka/Kinesis/CDC) via gRPC service

### Future Adoption (Trigger-Based)

| Tool | Enables | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| JAX | GPU autodiff, massive equation graphs | >100-parameter or >1000 QPS |
| PySR | symbolic discovery | enabled now as a primary discovery path |
| PyMC | full Bayesian | strong priors + small n |
| DuckDB/Postgres | analytical queries | large multi-tenant analytics |
| Vector DB | semantic equation retrieval | >100k equations |
| Graph DB | advanced multi-hop | >10k equation nodes |
| Numba | hot loop JIT | profiled bottleneck |


## 8. Architecture Extensibility Points

`
Layer 3 -> Alternative Fitter Slot
  Default: SciPy least_squares
  Advanced: JAX optimizer
  Primary: PySR (parallel discovery, enabled now)

Layer 5 -> Uncertainty Engine Slot
  Default: Laplace + Bootstrap
  Advanced: PyMC MCMC
  Future: Variational Inference

Layer 4 -> Differentiation Backend Slot
  Default: SymPy
  Advanced: JAX autodiff

Persistence Slot
  Default: SQLite
  Advanced: Postgres
  Future: Vector DB
`

This keeps the core boring, while allowing measured upgrades.

## 9. Data Flow Walkthrough

**Query**: "If we raise price by 10%, how does revenue change?"

**Observations**: 30 rows of (Price, Revenue, CAC, Churn).

**Layer 1**: Extract variables + units only.
**Layer 2**: Correlation check, partial correlation, drift check.
**Layer 3**: Fit canonical families, select lowest AIC, store equation.
**Layer 4**: Evaluate equation and derivatives.
**Layer 5**: Generate uncertainty distribution with model averaging.
**Layer 6**: Confidence gate checks thresholds.
**Layer 7**: Formatter produces prose, no reasoning.

**Output**: Answer + CI + sensitivity + trace + warnings.

## 10. Failure Modes & Fallback Router

### Hard Gate Behavior (Non-Configurable)
- n < threshold -> insufficient data
- R2_adj below threshold -> reject
- holdout MAPE above threshold -> reject
- drift detected -> reject or warn
- conflict detected -> reject or require review

### Fallback Router (Never Silent)

If the gate fails:
- n too small -> return data collection plan (missing observations list)
- no valid equation -> human review queue; PySR already ran as primary
- drift detected -> structural change alert with before/after comparison
- conflict exists -> resolution workflow with competing equations
- correlation too weak -> explicit "no detectable relationship"
- system error -> degraded mode: LLM answer labeled "not derived"

Critical rule: every failure returns an explainable next step.

## 11. Security & Privacy Architecture

- **Credentials**: read-only enforced. OAuth where possible. Secrets stored in vault.
- **Data residency**: self-hosted VPC option. Cloud option: encrypted at rest + in transit.
- **PII**: entity hashing and minimization. Observations store values, not personal identifiers.
- **Audit**: every sync + derivation logged in the Epistemic Ledger.
- **Access control**: RBAC (Admin, Analyst, Viewer).
- **API security**: rate limiting, idempotency keys, request signing.

## 12. Implementation Roadmap — All Phases

### Phase 1 — Core Engine (Weeks 1-2)
- Domain router + ontology
- Layer 1-6 core pipeline
- SQLite persistence
- Epistemic Ledger

### Phase 2 — Observation Fabric (Weeks 3-4)
- File upload (CSV/Excel)
- Schema Interpreter
- Observation Factory
- Quality Gate

### Phase 3 — Connectors (Weeks 5-6)
- Postgres/MySQL adapters
- SaaS adapters (Stripe, Salesforce, HubSpot)
- Incremental sync + CDC

### Phase 4 — Equation Graph + Conflict System (Weeks 7-8)
- Multi-hop inference
- Conflict detection
- Equation versioning + shadow evaluation

### Phase 5 — Reliability & Scale (Weeks 9-10)
- Valkey cache/queue
- Rate limiting + quotas
- Analytics on equation library

### Phase 6 — Final Phase (Weeks 11-12)
- Dashboard
- Docker images
- Kubernetes manifests
- Auth / RBAC UI

## 13. Final Phase — Dashboard, Docker, Kubernetes, Auth

These features are explicitly scheduled last to avoid premature ops complexity:

- **Dashboard**: equation health, drift alerts, data freshness, confidence distribution.
- **Docker**: production container images for API + worker.
- **Kubernetes**: Helm charts or manifests for scaling.
- **Auth**: SSO, RBAC enforcement, audit trail UI.

## 14. Redis Replacement — Why Valkey

Redis paid plans are not acceptable here. The production-grade free alternative is **Valkey**:

- **Valkey**: Linux Foundation fork of Redis 7.2, fully compatible, Apache 2.0 licensed.
- Backed by AWS, Google, Oracle, Ericsson.
- Drop-in replacement for caching, queues, and pub-sub.

Secondary free alternatives (if needed):
- **KeyDB** (BSD-3) — multithreaded Redis fork
- **Dragonfly** — high-performance Redis-compatible server
- **Garnet** — Microsoft open-source Redis alternative

## 15. Summary — What This System Is

The Equation Layer is a **production-grade reasoning core** that:

- Derives quantitative answers from equations, not text retrieval
- Forces mathematical validation and uncertainty at every step
- Never lets LLMs decide equation forms
- Learns continuously from observations and refits
- Produces auditable outputs with explicit trace and confidence

This is the foundation for reliable AI reasoning in quantitative domains.

37	│                        │    - Nonlinear least squares (scipy.optimize)
38	│                        │    - Compute R²_adj, AIC, holdout MAPE
39	│                        │    - Residual analysis: pattern? → reject.
40	│                        │  Select winner. Validate on HOLDOUT fold.
41	│                        │  If no candidate passes → reject all.
42	│                        │  Equation stored as SymPy AST + fitted params.
43	└───────────┬────────────┘
44	            │ {validated equation, params, stats, residuals}
45	            ▼
46	┌────────────────────────┐
47	│  LAYER 4               │

49	│  (Derivatives)         │  → Evaluate answer at input values
50	│                        │  → Symbolic derivatives (SymPy.diff)
51	│                        │  → Sensitivity map: ∂f/∂xᵢ at point
52	│                        │  → Curvature: ∂²f/∂xᵢ² at point
53	│                        │  → Cross-partials: ∂²f/∂xᵢ∂xⱼ

62	│                        │    - Gaussian N(θ*, Σ) around MAP estimate

64	│                        │    - Output: E[answer], σ, 95% CI, tail risk

69	│                        │    - Posterior predictive distribution
70	│                        │    - Use when priors are strong/domain-validated
71	│                        │

73	│                        │    E[answer] = ∫ f(θ) · p(θ) dθ
74	│                        │    Preserved: path context, how variables arrived

81	│  Formatter             │    n ≥ 15, R²_adj ≥ 0.6, holdout MAPE ≤ 20%,
82	│                        │    posterior width below threshold,
83	│                        │    no drift flag, no conflict flag.

92	**Layer 5 — Probabilistic Integrator** is the reinserted v1 component.
93	- **Default**: Laplace approximation — Gaussian around the fitted MAP estimate. Fast, assumption-light, no sampler tuning.
94	- **Upgrade path**: Full Bayesian via PyMC when the domain has strong, validated priors that justify the engineering cost.
95	
96	---
97	
98	## 2. The Integration — Restored, Modernized
99	
100	In v1, integration was a full Bayesian pipeline that dominated Layer 5. In v3, it is a **two-tier system**:
101	
102	### Tier 1: Laplace Approximation (default, always-on)
103	After the fitter returns parameters `θ*` and covariance `Σ`:
104	1. Define `q(θ) = N(θ*; Σ)` — a multivariate Gaussian centered at the MAP.
105	2. Draw 10,000 samples from `q(θ)`.
106	3. Evaluate `f(θ_sample)` for each.
107	4. Derive: mean, standard deviation, 95% credible interval, P(answer > k).
108	
109	**Why this is integration**: We are integrating the function over the parameter uncertainty distribution. The output is a full distribution over answers, not a single point. Same mathematical result as Bayesian inference, but in milliseconds instead of minutes.
110	
111	### Tier 2: Full Bayesian via PyMC (opt-in, per-equation)
112	When a domain expert supplies strong priors (e.g., "price elasticity is normally around -1.5 in our industry"):
113	1. Encode priors: `E ~ N(-1.5, 0.5)`.
114	2. Run NUTS sampler via PyMC.
115	3. Check convergence: R-hat < 1.01, ESS > 400.
116	4. Posterior predictive: `p(answer | data) = ∫ p(answer | θ) · p(θ | data) dθ`.
117	
118	**Trigger condition**: only when prior knowledge is strong and sample size is small (`n < 30`), where regularization from priors outweighs the MCMC overhead.
119	
120	---

122	## 3. Stack — Production Core (v2) + Advancement Roadmap

126	| Component | Choice | Why |
127	|---|---|---|
128	| Symbolic math | SymPy | Exact derivatives, equation composition, no GPU dependency |
129	| Optimization | scipy.optimize | L-BFGS-B, trust-region: robust, fast, 0-latency |
130	| Probabilistic integration | scipy.stats, numpy | Laplace approximation + bootstrap as default |
131	| Persistence | SQLite + JSONB | Zero operational overhead, structured equation rows |
132	| LLM layer | OpenAI/Anthropic API | Schema-constrained extraction |
133	| Correlation | numpy/scipy.stats | Partial correlation, drift detection |
134	| Graph queries | NetworkX | Multi-hop equation chains |
135	| Epistemic ledger | SQLite append-only log | Audit trail |
136	
137	### Future Advancement Map

139	The following tools are **architected for, but not required on day one**. Each has a clear adoption trigger.
140	
141	| Tool / Technology | What It Enables | Adoption Trigger |
142	|---|---|---|
143	| **JAX** | GPU-accelerated autodiff, JIT compilation, massive-scale gradients | >100-parameter equations, real-time inference at 1000+ QPS, or neural-hybrid models |

146	| **Chroma / Pinecone / Vector DB** | Semantic pre-filtering across millions of equations in a multi-tenant deployment | >100K active equations, distributed query, need for approximate nearest-neighbor search |
147	| **DuckDB / Postgres** | Columnar/relational analytics on equation library, cross-equation aggregation | Complex analytical queries over equation metadata at scale |
148	| **Graph DB (Neo4j)** | Native multi-hop causal reasoning, cycle detection in equation graph | Equation graph grows beyond 10K nodes, requires complex pathfinding |
149	| **Numba** | JIT compilation of hot numerical loops | Profiled bottleneck in bootstrap or residual analysis |
150	
151	---
152	
153	## 4. Why Each Advanced Tool Is Worth Keeping on the Roadmap
154	
155	| Tool | The Problem It Solves | Why Not Day 1 |
156	|---|---|---|
157	| **JAX** | v2's SymPy + numerical evaluation is O(1ms) for scalar equations. JAX becomes necessary when you differentiate through *compositions* of 100+ equations or batch-evaluate 10,000 parameter configurations simultaneously. | Adds GPU dependency, binary size, JIT warmup latency. SymPy handles 99% of quantitative business questions. |
158	| **PySR** | Sometimes the true relationship is genuinely unknown (e.g., material science, biological systems). LLM cannot propose a form it has never seen. PySR explores function space systematically. | Stochastic search; can overfit noise; adds minutes of latency. For business/finance relationships, LLM + structured validation is usually sufficient. |
159	| **PyMC** | When you have 8 observations and a domain expert who *knows* the prior, full Bayesian inference extracts signal that frequentist methods cannot. | Requires prior engineering, MCMC diagnostics, convergence checking. Laplace approximation gives the same interval for well-behaved posteriors at 1/1000th the cost. |
160	| **Vector DB** | When you have 1 million equations across 500 customers, SQL exact match by variable set is still instant (<5ms). Vector similarity only helps when exact variable names differ but semantic meaning overlaps. | Exact variable matching is more reliable for equations. Semantic drift in vector space can return structurally wrong matches. |
161	
162	---
163	
164	## 5. Architecture Extensibility Points
165	
166	The v3 pipeline has **defined extension slots** where advanced tools plug in without rewriting the core:
167	
168	```
169	LAYER 3 — Fitter + Validator
170	  └── Extension Slot A: Alternative Fitter
171	      ├── Default: scipy.optimize.least_squares
172	      ├── Advanced: JAX-opt (GPU-accelerated, large parameter spaces)
173	      └── Fallback: PySR (when no candidate fits)
174	
175	LAYER 5 — Probabilistic Integrator
176	  └── Extension Slot B: Uncertainty Engine
177	      ├── Default: Laplace approximation + Bootstrap
178	      ├── Advanced: PyMC (full MCMC when priors justify it)
179	      └── Future: Variational Inference (PyMC ADVI for very large posteriors)
180	
181	LAYER 4 — Computational Core
182	  └── Extension Slot C: Differentiation Backend
183	      ├── Default: SymPy exact + numerical evaluation
184	      ├── Advanced: JAX autodiff (for neural-symbolic hybrids)
185	      └── Future: Forward-mode autodiff (for R^n → R functions)
186	
187	PERSISTENCE
188	  └── Extension Slot D: Storage Backend
189	      ├── Default: SQLite (single-node, zero ops)
190	      ├── Advanced: PostgreSQL (multi-tenant, concurrent writes)
191	      └── Future: Vector DB (semantic pre-filtering at massive scale)
192	```
193	
194	---
195	
196	## 6. Data Flow (with Integration Restored)
197	
198	**Query**: "If we raise price by 10%, how does revenue change?"
199	
200	**Observations**: 30 rows of (Price, Revenue, CAC, Churn).
201	
202	**Layer 1 — Epistemic Parser**
203	- Extracts: `Price (P)`, `Revenue (R)`, `CAC (C)`, `Churn (H)`.
204	- Proposes: `R = a·P + b`, `R = a·P^b`, `R = a·exp(b·P)`.
205	
206	**Layer 2 — Correlation Engine**
207	- `ρ(P, R) = -0.74`. Partial `ρ(P, R|C) = -0.61`. Pass.
208	
209	**Layer 3 — Fitter + Validator**
210	- `power` wins: `R = 2.1M · P^(-1.4)`, R²=0.82, holdout MAPE=8%.
211	- Params: `a = 2.1M (σ=0.12M)`, `b = -1.4 (σ=0.18)`.
212	
213	**Layer 4 — Computational Core**
214	- `P = 110` → `R = 284,500`.
215	- `dR/dP = -2,580`. `d²R/dP² > 0` (decelerating).
216	
217	**Layer 5 — Probabilistic Integrator (REINSERTED)**
218	- **Laplace mode** (default):
219	  - Draw 10,000 samples from `N([2.1M, -1.4], Σ)`.
220	  - Evaluate `R(θ_sample)` at `P = 110` for each.
221	  - Result: `E[R] = 284,500`, `σ = 18,200`, `CI = [252,300, 319,100]`.
222	  - Tail risk: `P(R > 300,000) = 19%`.
223	- **PyMC mode** (if activated for this equation):
224	  - Priors: `a ~ N(2M, 0.5M)`, `b ~ N(-1.5, 0.5)`.
225	  - NUTS sampler: 4 chains × 1000 draws.
226	  - Converged: R-hat = 1.002, ESS = 1,847.
227	  - Posterior predictive at `P = 110`: same mean, slightly wider CI due to prior regularization.
228	
229	**Layer 6 — Confidence Gate & Formatter**
230	- All thresholds pass → formatted output with full uncertainty profile.
231	
232	---
233	
234	## 7. Summary
235	
236	v3 retains everything from v2:
237	- 6 layers (was 5 — Integration is back as Layer 5)
238	- Holdout validation, Equation Graph, Conflict Detector, Epistemic Ledger
239	- Bootstrap as fast default uncertainty method
240	
241	And restores the computational ambition of v1:
242	- **Probabilistic Integration** is a first-class layer, not removed
243	- **Laplace approximation** is the default, making uncertainty quantification instant
244	- **Full Bayesian (PyMC)** is an opt-in upgrade mode for small-data + strong-prior scenarios
245	- **JAX, PySR, PyMC, Vector DB** are all defined on the advancement roadmap with clear adoption triggers
246	
247	The system is **boring by default, powerful by extension**. It ships fast, proves value, then upgrades component by component based on measured need.

# Equation Layer v3 — Production Core + Future Roadmap

> **Constraint**: LLM extracts variables *and* proposes equations. No pre-defined library.
> **Goal**: v2 reliability + v1 computational power + clear upgrade path.

---

## 1. Architecture — 6 Layers

```
QUERY + OBSERVATIONS
        │
        ▼
┌────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 1               │
│  Epistemic Parser      │  LLM with strict JSON schema:
│  (Hypothesis Generator)│  → Extract variables, values, units, domains
│                        │  → Propose 1-3 candidate equation forms
│                        │  → Tag causal direction (associational)
│                        │  → Flag any stated assumptions
└───────────┬────────────┘
            │ {candidates, variables, context}
            ▼
┌────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 2               │
│  Correlation Engine    │  Fast matrix: ρ(xᵢ, xⱼ) for all pairs.
│  + Drift Detector      │  Partial correlation strips confounders.
│                        │  Drift: flag if historical ρ differs from
│                        │  current window. Structural break signal.
│                        │  → Block fit if ρ < threshold for claimed pair
└───────────┬────────────┘
            │ {validated candidates, correlation map, drift flags}
            ▼
┌────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 3               │
│  Fitter + Validator    │  For each candidate form:
│                        │    - Nonlinear least squares (scipy.optimize)
│                        │    - Compute R²_adj, AIC, holdout MAPE
│                        │    - Residual analysis: pattern? → reject.
│                        │  Select winner. Validate on HOLDOUT fold.
│                        │  If no candidate passes → reject all.
│                        │  Equation stored as SymPy AST + fitted params.
└───────────┬────────────┘
            │ {validated equation, params, stats, residuals}
            ▼
┌────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 4               │
│  Computational Core    │  Symbolic + numerical math, zero ML:
│  (Derivatives)         │  → Evaluate answer at input values
│                        │  → Symbolic derivatives (SymPy.diff)
│                        │  → Sensitivity map: ∂f/∂xᵢ at point
│                        │  → Curvature: ∂²f/∂xᵢ² at point
│                        │  → Cross-partials: ∂²f/∂xᵢ∂xⱼ
└───────────┬────────────┘
            │ {answer, derivatives, equation}
            ▼
┌────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 5               │
│  Probabilistic         │  Parameters are distributions, not points.
│  Integrator            │  
│  (Reinserted)          │  Primary (default): Laplace Approximation
│                        │    - Gaussian N(θ*, Σ) around MAP estimate
│                        │    - Sample parameters, evaluate f(θ)
│                        │    - Output: E[answer], σ, 95% CI, tail risk
│                        │  
│                        │  Upgrade (optional): Full Bayesian via PyMC
│                        │    - Specify priors on parameters
│                        │    - MCMC chains (NUTS sampler)
│                        │    - Posterior predictive distribution
│                        │    - Use when priors are strong/domain-validated
│                        │  
│                        │  Integration over uncertainty:
│                        │    E[answer] = ∫ f(θ) · p(θ) dθ
│                        │    Preserved: path context, how variables arrived
└───────────┬────────────┘
            │ {answer, uncertainty, credibility, tail risk}
            ▼
┌────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 6               │
│  Confidence Gate &     │  Hard thresholds (non-configurable):
│  Formatter             │    n ≥ 15, R²_adj ≥ 0.6, holdout MAPE ≤ 20%,
│                        │    posterior width below threshold,
│                        │    no drift flag, no conflict flag.
│                        │  Fail any → structured "insufficient data".
│                        │  Pass all → minimal LLM formats to prose.
└───────────┬────────────┘
            │
            ▼
         OUTPUT
```

**Layer 5 — Probabilistic Integrator** is the reinserted v1 component.
- **Default**: Laplace approximation — Gaussian around the fitted MAP estimate. Fast, assumption-light, no sampler tuning.
- **Upgrade path**: Full Bayesian via PyMC when the domain has strong, validated priors that justify the engineering cost.

---

## 2. The Integration — Restored, Modernized

In v1, integration was a full Bayesian pipeline that dominated Layer 5. In v3, it is a **two-tier system**:

### Tier 1: Laplace Approximation (default, always-on)
After the fitter returns parameters `θ*` and covariance `Σ`:
1. Define `q(θ) = N(θ*; Σ)` — a multivariate Gaussian centered at the MAP.
2. Draw 10,000 samples from `q(θ)`.
3. Evaluate `f(θ_sample)` for each.
4. Derive: mean, standard deviation, 95% credible interval, P(answer > k).

**Why this is integration**: We are integrating the function over the parameter uncertainty distribution. The output is a full distribution over answers, not a single point. Same mathematical result as Bayesian inference, but in milliseconds instead of minutes.

### Tier 2: Full Bayesian via PyMC (opt-in, per-equation)
When a domain expert supplies strong priors (e.g., "price elasticity is normally around -1.5 in our industry"):
1. Encode priors: `E ~ N(-1.5, 0.5)`.
2. Run NUTS sampler via PyMC.
3. Check convergence: R-hat < 1.01, ESS > 400.
4. Posterior predictive: `p(answer | data) = ∫ p(answer | θ) · p(θ | data) dθ`.

**Trigger condition**: only when prior knowledge is strong and sample size is small (`n < 30`), where regularization from priors outweighs the MCMC overhead.

---

## 3. Stack — Production Core (v2) + Advancement Roadmap

### Current Production Stack (Day 1)

| Component | Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Symbolic math | SymPy | Exact derivatives, equation composition, no GPU dependency |
| Optimization | scipy.optimize | L-BFGS-B, trust-region: robust, fast, 0-latency |
| Probabilistic integration | scipy.stats, numpy | Laplace approximation + bootstrap as default |
| Persistence | SQLite + JSONB | Zero operational overhead, structured equation rows |
| LLM layer | OpenAI/Anthropic API | Schema-constrained extraction |
| Correlation | numpy/scipy.stats | Partial correlation, drift detection |
| Graph queries | NetworkX | Multi-hop equation chains |
| Epistemic ledger | SQLite append-only log | Audit trail |

### Future Advancement Map

The following tools are **architected for, but not required on day one**. Each has a clear adoption trigger.

| Tool / Technology | What It Enables | Adoption Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **JAX** | GPU-accelerated autodiff, JIT compilation, massive-scale gradients | >100-parameter equations, real-time inference at 1000+ QPS, or neural-hybrid models |
| **PySR** | Automated equation discovery when LLM cannot propose forms | Zero candidates pass validation repeatedly; domain has unknown governing laws |
| **PyMC (full Bayesian)** | Hierarchical models, complex priors, scientific-grade uncertainty | Small data + strong priors; publication-grade inference requirements |
| **Chroma / Pinecone / Vector DB** | Semantic pre-filtering across millions of equations in a multi-tenant deployment | >100K active equations, distributed query, need for approximate nearest-neighbor search |
| **DuckDB / Postgres** | Columnar/relational analytics on equation library, cross-equation aggregation | Complex analytical queries over equation metadata at scale |
| **Graph DB (Neo4j)** | Native multi-hop causal reasoning, cycle detection in equation graph | Equation graph grows beyond 10K nodes, requires complex pathfinding |
| **Numba** | JIT compilation of hot numerical loops | Profiled bottleneck in bootstrap or residual analysis |

---

## 4. Why Each Advanced Tool Is Worth Keeping on the Roadmap

| Tool | The Problem It Solves | Why Not Day 1 |
|---|---|---|
| **JAX** | v2's SymPy + numerical evaluation is O(1ms) for scalar equations. JAX becomes necessary when you differentiate through *compositions* of 100+ equations or batch-evaluate 10,000 parameter configurations simultaneously. | Adds GPU dependency, binary size, JIT warmup latency. SymPy handles 99% of quantitative business questions. |
| **PySR** | Sometimes the true relationship is genuinely unknown (e.g., material science, biological systems). LLM cannot propose a form it has never seen. PySR explores function space systematically. | Stochastic search; can overfit noise; adds minutes of latency. For business/finance relationships, LLM + structured validation is usually sufficient. |
| **PyMC** | When you have 8 observations and a domain expert who *knows* the prior, full Bayesian inference extracts signal that frequentist methods cannot. | Requires prior engineering, MCMC diagnostics, convergence checking. Laplace approximation gives the same interval for well-behaved posteriors at 1/1000th the cost. |
| **Vector DB** | When you have 1 million equations across 500 customers, SQL exact match by variable set is still instant (<5ms). Vector similarity only helps when exact variable names differ but semantic meaning overlaps. | Exact variable matching is more reliable for equations. Semantic drift in vector space can return structurally wrong matches. |

---

## 5. Architecture Extensibility Points

The v3 pipeline has **defined extension slots** where advanced tools plug in without rewriting the core:

```
LAYER 3 — Fitter + Validator
  └── Extension Slot A: Alternative Fitter
      ├── Default: scipy.optimize.least_squares
      ├── Advanced: JAX-opt (GPU-accelerated, large parameter spaces)
      └── Fallback: PySR (when no candidate fits)

LAYER 5 — Probabilistic Integrator
  └── Extension Slot B: Uncertainty Engine
      ├── Default: Laplace approximation + Bootstrap
      ├── Advanced: PyMC (full MCMC when priors justify it)
      └── Future: Variational Inference (PyMC ADVI for very large posteriors)

LAYER 4 — Computational Core
  └── Extension Slot C: Differentiation Backend
      ├── Default: SymPy exact + numerical evaluation
      ├── Advanced: JAX autodiff (for neural-symbolic hybrids)
      └── Future: Forward-mode autodiff (for R^n → R functions)

PERSISTENCE
  └── Extension Slot D: Storage Backend
      ├── Default: SQLite (single-node, zero ops)
      ├── Advanced: PostgreSQL (multi-tenant, concurrent writes)
      └── Future: Vector DB (semantic pre-filtering at massive scale)
```

---

## 6. Data Flow (with Integration Restored)

**Query**: "If we raise price by 10%, how does revenue change?"

**Observations**: 30 rows of (Price, Revenue, CAC, Churn).

**Layer 1 — Epistemic Parser**
- Extracts: `Price (P)`, `Revenue (R)`, `CAC (C)`, `Churn (H)`.
- Proposes: `R = a·P + b`, `R = a·P^b`, `R = a·exp(b·P)`.

**Layer 2 — Correlation Engine**
- `ρ(P, R) = -0.74`. Partial `ρ(P, R|C) = -0.61`. Pass.

**Layer 3 — Fitter + Validator**
- `power` wins: `R = 2.1M · P^(-1.4)`, R²=0.82, holdout MAPE=8%.
- Params: `a = 2.1M (σ=0.12M)`, `b = -1.4 (σ=0.18)`.

**Layer 4 — Computational Core**
- `P = 110` → `R = 284,500`.
- `dR/dP = -2,580`. `d²R/dP² > 0` (decelerating).

**Layer 5 — Probabilistic Integrator (REINSERTED)**
- **Laplace mode** (default):
  - Draw 10,000 samples from `N([2.1M, -1.4], Σ)`.
  - Evaluate `R(θ_sample)` at `P = 110` for each.
  - Result: `E[R] = 284,500`, `σ = 18,200`, `CI = [252,300, 319,100]`.
  - Tail risk: `P(R > 300,000) = 19%`.
- **PyMC mode** (if activated for this equation):
  - Priors: `a ~ N(2M, 0.5M)`, `b ~ N(-1.5, 0.5)`.
  - NUTS sampler: 4 chains × 1000 draws.
  - Converged: R-hat = 1.002, ESS = 1,847.
  - Posterior predictive at `P = 110`: same mean, slightly wider CI due to prior regularization.

**Layer 6 — Confidence Gate & Formatter**
- All thresholds pass → formatted output with full uncertainty profile.

---

## 7. Summary

v3 retains everything from v2:
- 6 layers (was 5 — Integration is back as Layer 5)
- Holdout validation, Equation Graph, Conflict Detector, Epistemic Ledger
- Bootstrap as fast default uncertainty method

And restores the computational ambition of v1:
- **Probabilistic Integration** is a first-class layer, not removed
- **Laplace approximation** is the default, making uncertainty quantification instant
- **Full Bayesian (PyMC)** is an opt-in upgrade mode for small-data + strong-prior scenarios
- **JAX, PySR, PyMC, Vector DB** are all defined on the advancement roadmap with clear adoption triggers

The system is **boring by default, powerful by extension**. It ships fast, proves value, then upgrades component by component based on measured need.

1	# Equation Layer — The Observation Fabric (Connector Architecture)
2	
3	> **Core Insight**: RAG connectors move *text chunks*. The Equation Layer connector moves *structured observations*. This is a fundamentally different ingestion problem — and a bigger product opportunity.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Why "Like RAG" Is the Wrong Starting Point
8	
9	| Aspect | RAG Connector | Equation Layer Connector |
10	|---|---|---|
11	| **Raw input** | Documents, PDFs, web pages | CSVs, SQL tables, APIs, tool exports |
12	| **Unit of ingestion** | Text chunk (~500 tokens) | Observation: `{variable, value, timestamp, entity, unit, context}` |
13	| **Transformation** | Chunk + embed | Schema map + type infer + normalize + validate |
14	| **Storage** | Vector DB (semantic search) | Observation ledger + Equation library (structured) |
15	| **Update trigger** | Manual re-index | Live sync, incremental, or event-driven |
16	| **User work** | Drop files, done | Drop files → confirm variable mapping → validate → done |
17	
18	RAG connectors are passive pipes. The Equation Layer connector is an **active observation system** — it doesn't just store data, it continuously transforms raw business data into structured epistemic inputs that feed the equation pipeline.
19	
20	---
21	
22	## 2. The Observation Fabric — 5-Layer Connector Architecture
23	
24	```
25	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
26	│  SOURCE LAYER                                               │
27	│  CSV  │  SQL DB  │  SaaS API  │  Spreadsheet  │  Streaming  │
28	└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘
29	                     │ raw records
30	                     ▼
31	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
32	│  ADAPTER LAYER                                              │
33	│  ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐           │
34	│  │ CSV     │ │ SQL     │ │ REST    │ │ Webhook │           │
35	│  │ Adapter │ │ Adapter │ │ Adapter │ │ Adapter │           │
36	│  └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘           │
37	│  → Unified record stream: {columns, types, sample_rows}     │
38	└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘
39	                     │ unified records
40	                     ▼
41	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
42	│  SCHEMA INTERPRETER                                         │
43	│  LLM-powered or rule-based mapping from source schema to    │
44	│  Equation Layer variable ontology.                          │
45	│  → "revenue_q2" → Variable: Revenue, Unit: USD,             │
46	│     Entity: Company, Period: quarterly                      │
47	│  → "cust_acq_cost" → Variable: CAC, Unit: USD,              │
48	│     Entity: CustomerSegment, Period: monthly                │
49	│  User confirms or overrides. One-time per source.           │
50	└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘
51	                     │ mapped variables
52	                     ▼
53	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
54	│  OBSERVATION FACTORY                                        │
55	│  Transforms each source record into 1-N observations:       │
56	│  {                                                          │
57	│    variable_id: "revenue",                                  │
58	│    value: 2845000.00,                                       │
59	│    unit: "USD",                                             │
60	│    timestamp: "2024-03-31T00:00:00Z",                       │
61	│    entity: {"company": "AcmeCorp", "region": "US"},         │
62	│    provenance: {source: "stripe_api", record_id: "..."},    │
63	│    quality_score: 1.0                                       │
64	│  }                                                          │
65	│  → Handles: type coercion, unit normalization, currency     │
66	│    conversion, outlier flagging, missing value policies     │
67	└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘
68	                     │ observations
69	                     ▼
70	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
71	│  SYNC ENGINE + QUALITY GATE                                 │
72	│  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐         │
73	│  │ Batch Sync  │  │ Incremental │  │ Event-Driven│         │
74	│  │ (full load) │  │ (delta)     │  │ (webhooks)  │         │
75	│  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘         │
76	│  → Deduplication by (variable, entity, timestamp)           │
77	│  → Data quality checks: range, variance, schema drift       │
78	│  → Auto-trigger: new observations → equation update queue   │
79	└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘
80	                     │ validated observations
81	                     ▼
82	              ┌──────────────┐
83	              │  EQUATION    │
84	              │  LAYER CORE  │
85	              │  (Layers 1-6)│
86	              └──────────────┘
87	```
88	
89	---
90	
91	## 3. Connection Types — The User Experience
92	
93	### 3.1 File Upload (CSV, Excel, Parquet)
94	
95	**Flow**:
96	1. User drags file.
97	2. Adapter auto-detects delimiter, encoding, header row.
98	3. Schema Interpreter suggests variable mappings:
99	   ```
100	   Column "rev_2024_q1" → Revenue (USD, quarterly)
101	   Column "cac_avg" → CAC (USD, monthly average)
102	   Column "churn_pct" → ChurnRate (percentage, monthly)
103	   ```
104	4. User confirms or edits mappings in a UI.
105	5. Observation Factory generates observations.
106	6. Quality Gate flags: "Revenue has 3 negative values — confirm refunds or data error?"
107	7. Data enters equation pipeline.
108	
109	**Advanced**: Support for multi-sheet Excel, Parquet with nested types, JSONL with schema inference.
110	
111	### 3.2 Database Connector (Postgres, MySQL, BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift)
112	
113	**Flow**:
114	1. User provides read-only connection string (or uses OAuth for cloud warehouses).
115	2. Adapter introspects schema: `SHOW TABLES`, `DESCRIBE`.
116	3. Schema Interpreter suggests relevant tables and columns.
117	4. User selects tables/columns to sync.
118	5. Sync Engine performs initial full load, then incremental via `updated_at` timestamp or CDC (Change Data Capture).
119	6. Observations stream continuously into the ledger.
120	
121	**Security**: Read-only credentials enforced. Query whitelisting. No raw data leaves user's VPC for self-hosted deployments.
122	
123	### 3.3 SaaS Tool Connectors (Stripe, Salesforce, HubSpot, QuickBooks, etc.)
124	
125	**Pre-built adapters** for common business tools:
126	- **Stripe**: `charges`, `subscriptions`, `invoices` → Revenue, MRR, Churn
127	- **Salesforce**: `opportunities`, `accounts` → Pipeline, ACV, WinRate
128	- **HubSpot**: `contacts`, `deals` → Leads, ConversionRate, CAC
129	- **QuickBooks/Xero**: `profit_loss`, `balance_sheet` → Revenue, COGS, Opex
130	- **Google Analytics**: `traffic`, `conversions` → Visitors, ConversionRate, BounceRate
131	- **Mixpanel/Amplitude**: `events`, `funnels` → DAU, Retention, FeatureAdoption
132	
133	**Flow**:
134	1. OAuth authentication.
135	2. Adapter fetches standard endpoints.
136	3. Schema Interpreter maps API response fields to variables.
137	4. Incremental sync via API pagination + cursor.
138	5. Observations generated with tool-native provenance.
139	
140	### 3.4 API / Webhook (Custom Pushes)
141	
142	For users who want to push data programmatically:
143	
144	```bash
145	POST /v1/observations
146	{
147	  "source": "internal_etl",
148	  "observations": [
149	    {
150	      "variable": "revenue",

# Equation Layer — The Observation Fabric (Connector Architecture)

> **Core Insight**: RAG connectors move *text chunks*. The Equation Layer connector moves *structured observations*. This is a fundamentally different ingestion problem — and a bigger product opportunity.

---

## 1. Why "Like RAG" Is the Wrong Starting Point

| Aspect | RAG Connector | Equation Layer Connector |
|---|---|---|
| **Raw input** | Documents, PDFs, web pages | CSVs, SQL tables, APIs, tool exports |
| **Unit of ingestion** | Text chunk (~500 tokens) | Observation: `{variable, value, timestamp, entity, unit, context}` |
| **Transformation** | Chunk + embed | Schema map + type infer + normalize + validate |
| **Storage** | Vector DB (semantic search) | Observation ledger + Equation library (structured) |
| **Update trigger** | Manual re-index | Live sync, incremental, or event-driven |
| **User work** | Drop files, done | Drop files → confirm variable mapping → validate → done |

RAG connectors are passive pipes. The Equation Layer connector is an **active observation system** — it doesn't just store data, it continuously transforms raw business data into structured epistemic inputs that feed the equation pipeline.

---

## 2. The Observation Fabric — 5-Layer Connector Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SOURCE LAYER                                               │
│  CSV  │  SQL DB  │  SaaS API  │  Spreadsheet  │  Streaming  │
└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘
                     │ raw records
                     ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ADAPTER LAYER                                              │
│  ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐           │
│  │ CSV     │ │ SQL     │ │ REST    │ │ Webhook │           │
│  │ Adapter │ │ Adapter │ │ Adapter │ │ Adapter │           │
│  └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘           │
│  → Unified record stream: {columns, types, sample_rows}     │
└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘
                     │ unified records
                     ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SCHEMA INTERPRETER                                         │
│  LLM-powered or rule-based mapping from source schema to    │
│  Equation Layer variable ontology.                          │
│  → "revenue_q2" → Variable: Revenue, Unit: USD,             │
│     Entity: Company, Period: quarterly                      │
│  → "cust_acq_cost" → Variable: CAC, Unit: USD,              │
│     Entity: CustomerSegment, Period: monthly                │
│  User confirms or overrides. One-time per source.           │
└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘
                     │ mapped variables
                     ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  OBSERVATION FACTORY                                        │
│  Transforms each source record into 1-N observations:       │
│  {                                                          │
│    variable_id: "revenue",                                  │
│    value: 2845000.00,                                       │
│    unit: "USD",                                             │
│    timestamp: "2024-03-31T00:00:00Z",                       │
│    entity: {"company": "AcmeCorp", "region": "US"},         │
│    provenance: {source: "stripe_api", record_id: "..."},    │
│    quality_score: 1.0                                       │
│  }                                                          │
│  → Handles: type coercion, unit normalization, currency     │
│    conversion, outlier flagging, missing value policies     │
└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘
                     │ observations
                     ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SYNC ENGINE + QUALITY GATE                                 │
│  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐         │
│  │ Batch Sync  │  │ Incremental │  │ Event-Driven│         │
│  │ (full load) │  │ (delta)     │  │ (webhooks)  │         │
│  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘         │
│  → Deduplication by (variable, entity, timestamp)           │
│  → Data quality checks: range, variance, schema drift       │
│  → Auto-trigger: new observations → equation update queue   │
└────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘
                     │ validated observations
                     ▼
              ┌──────────────┐
              │  EQUATION    │
              │  LAYER CORE  │
              │  (Layers 1-6)│
              └──────────────┘
```

---

## 3. Connection Types — The User Experience

### 3.1 File Upload (CSV, Excel, Parquet)

**Flow**:
1. User drags file.
2. Adapter auto-detects delimiter, encoding, header row.
3. Schema Interpreter suggests variable mappings:
   ```
   Column "rev_2024_q1" → Revenue (USD, quarterly)
   Column "cac_avg" → CAC (USD, monthly average)
   Column "churn_pct" → ChurnRate (percentage, monthly)
   ```
4. User confirms or edits mappings in a UI.
5. Observation Factory generates observations.
6. Quality Gate flags: "Revenue has 3 negative values — confirm refunds or data error?"
7. Data enters equation pipeline.

**Advanced**: Support for multi-sheet Excel, Parquet with nested types, JSONL with schema inference.

### 3.2 Database Connector (Postgres, MySQL, BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift)

**Flow**:
1. User provides read-only connection string (or uses OAuth for cloud warehouses).
2. Adapter introspects schema: `SHOW TABLES`, `DESCRIBE`.
3. Schema Interpreter suggests relevant tables and columns.
4. User selects tables/columns to sync.
5. Sync Engine performs initial full load, then incremental via `updated_at` timestamp or CDC (Change Data Capture).
6. Observations stream continuously into the ledger.

**Security**: Read-only credentials enforced. Query whitelisting. No raw data leaves user's VPC for self-hosted deployments.

### 3.3 SaaS Tool Connectors (Stripe, Salesforce, HubSpot, QuickBooks, etc.)

**Pre-built adapters** for common business tools:
- **Stripe**: `charges`, `subscriptions`, `invoices` → Revenue, MRR, Churn
- **Salesforce**: `opportunities`, `accounts` → Pipeline, ACV, WinRate
- **HubSpot**: `contacts`, `deals` → Leads, ConversionRate, CAC
- **QuickBooks/Xero**: `profit_loss`, `balance_sheet` → Revenue, COGS, Opex
- **Google Analytics**: `traffic`, `conversions` → Visitors, ConversionRate, BounceRate
- **Mixpanel/Amplitude**: `events`, `funnels` → DAU, Retention, FeatureAdoption

**Flow**:
1. OAuth authentication.
2. Adapter fetches standard endpoints.
3. Schema Interpreter maps API response fields to variables.
4. Incremental sync via API pagination + cursor.
5. Observations generated with tool-native provenance.

### 3.4 API / Webhook (Custom Pushes)

For users who want to push data programmatically:

```bash
POST /v1/observations
{
  "source": "internal_etl",
  "observations": [
    {
      "variable": "revenue",
      "value": 2845000,
      "unit": "USD",
      "timestamp": "2024-03-31T00:00:00Z",
      "entity": {"company": "AcmeCorp"}
    }
  ]
}
```

**Validation**: Schema validation, rate limiting, idempotency keys.

### 3.5 Streaming (Kafka, Kinesis, Pub/Sub)

For real-time use cases:
- Consumer reads from topic.
- Observation Factory transforms events in flight.
- Quality Gate operates on micro-batches.
- Equations update near-real-time (seconds latency).

---

## 4. The Schema Interpreter — The Secret Weapon

This is where the product becomes intelligent. RAG doesn't need to understand what a column means. The Equation Layer absolutely does.

### Auto-Mapping Pipeline

```
Input: ["rev_q1_2024", "cust_acq_cost", "mrr_total", "churn_rate_pct"]
       ↓
LLM Schema Interpreter (with few-shot examples)
       ↓
Output:
  rev_q1_2024 → Variable: Revenue, Unit: USD, Period: Q1-2024, Type: continuous
  cust_acq_cost → Variable: CAC, Unit: USD, Period: monthly, Type: continuous
  mrr_total → Variable: MRR, Unit: USD, Period: monthly, Type: continuous
  churn_rate_pct → Variable: ChurnRate, Unit: percentage, Period: monthly, Type: continuous
```

### Confidence Scoring
- High confidence: exact match to known variable synonyms (`revenue`, `sales`, `turnover`)
- Medium confidence: pattern match (`rev_*`, `*_q[1-4]_*`, `cac_*`)
- Low confidence: ambiguous (`value`, `metric_1`, `col_d`)

**User experience**: High-confidence mappings are auto-approved. Medium/low confidence are presented for one-click confirmation.

### Domain-Aware Ontology
The system maintains a variable ontology:
```
finance:
  revenue: {synonyms: ["sales", "turnover", "top_line"], unit: "currency", type: "continuous"}
  cac: {synonyms: ["customer_acquisition_cost", "acquisition_cost"], unit: "currency", type: "continuous"}
  ltv: {synonyms: ["lifetime_value", "clv"], unit: "currency", type: "continuous"}
  churn_rate: {synonyms: ["churn", "attrition"], unit: "percentage", type: "continuous"}
```

This ontology is **learned and extended** over time. When a user maps a new column manually, the system remembers the synonym.

---

## 5. The Observation Factory — Data Transformation

Raw records are rarely clean. The factory handles:

### 5.1 Type Coercion
- Strings that look like numbers: `"$1,234.56"` → `1234.56`
- Percentages: `"12.5%"` → `0.125`
- Dates: multiple formats normalized to ISO 8601

### 5.2 Unit Normalization
- Currency: detect currency code, convert to base currency if exchange rates provided
- Scale: `"2.3M"` → `2300000`, `"1.2K"` → `1200`
- Time: `"monthly"`, `"quarterly"`, `"annual"` tagged explicitly

### 5.3 Entity Resolution
- `"Acme Corp"`, `"AcmeCorp"`, `"ACME CORPORATION"` → canonical entity ID
- Uses fuzzy matching + user confirmation

### 5.4 Outlier Detection
- Z-score or IQR method flags extreme values
- User policy: reject, cap, or flag

### 5.5 Missing Value Policy
- Forward fill for time series
- Interpolation options
- Explicit `null` observation with `quality_score: 0.0`

---

## 6. Sync Engine — Keeping Equations Alive

The connector is not a one-time import. It is a **living data fabric**.

### Sync Modes

| Mode | Use Case | Latency |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch** | Monthly financial reports, quarterly filings | Hours |
| **Incremental** | Daily database sync, weekly SaaS pulls | Minutes |
| **Event-driven** | Stripe webhooks, transaction streams | Seconds |
| **On-demand** | User uploads a new CSV | Instant |

### Auto-Update Trigger
When new observations arrive for variables in an existing equation:
1. Queue equation for refit.
2. Run Layer 3 (Fitter) with old + new data.
3. Check if parameters drifted significantly.
4. If drift > threshold: flag equation for review, notify user.
5. If fit improves: update equation silently, log in Epistemic Ledger.

### The "Equation Health" Dashboard
Users see:
- Which equations are active
- Data freshness per equation ("Last updated 2 hours ago")
- Observation count trend
- Drift alerts
- Data quality issues

---

## 7. Data Quality Gate — Trust Through Validation

Before observations enter the equation pipeline, they pass:

```
1. Schema validation: correct types, required fields present
2. Range checks: Revenue should not be negative (unless configured)
3. Variance checks: sudden drop to zero = likely missing data, not true zero
4. Cross-field validation: CAC > LTV flagged as suspicious
5. Temporal checks: future dates rejected, gaps flagged
6. Duplication: (variable, entity, timestamp) must be unique
```

**Failed observations** go to a quarantine queue. User reviews and approves/rejects.

---

## 8. Security & Privacy Architecture

| Concern | Solution |
|---|---|
| **Database credentials** | Read-only enforced. OAuth where possible. No credential storage in plaintext — use vault (HashiCorp, AWS Secrets Manager). |
| **Data residency** | Self-hosted option: Equation Layer runs in user's VPC. Data never leaves. Cloud option: encrypted at rest and in transit. |
| **PII handling** | Entity resolution can hash identifiers. Observations store values, not personal data. |
| **Audit** | Every sync logged in Epistemic Ledger: who connected what, when, what changed. |
| **Access control** | Role-based: Admin (connect sources), Analyst (query equations), Viewer (see outputs). |

---

## 9. What Makes This Excellent (Not Just Functional)

### 9.1 Proactive, Not Passive
RAG waits for a query, then retrieves. The Observation Fabric **continuously improves equations** as data arrives. The system gets smarter without user intervention.

### 9.2 Domain-Aware from Day 1
The Schema Interpreter doesn't just parse — it *understands* business context. It knows that `mrr` and `arr` are related, that `cac` and `ltv` should be compared, that `churn` is a rate not a count.

### 9.3 Self-Healing Data Pipeline
When a source schema changes (column renamed, type changed), the system detects drift and alerts the user — rather than silently breaking equations.

### 9.4 The "Drop and Derive" Experience
User story:
1. Connect Stripe (OAuth, 30 seconds).
2. System auto-discovers: Revenue, MRR, Churn, CAC.
3. User asks: "If I reduce churn by 10%, how does LTV change?"
4. System already has the data. It derives the answer in seconds.

No manual CSV exports. No SQL writing. No data engineering.

---

## 10. Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: File Upload + CSV (Week 1-2)
- Drag-and-drop CSV/Excel
- Schema Interpreter with 50 common business variables
- Observation Factory with basic normalization
- SQLite persistence

### Phase 2: Database Connectors (Week 3-4)
- Postgres, MySQL adapters
- Schema introspection
- Incremental sync
- Connection manager UI

### Phase 3: SaaS Connectors (Week 5-6)
- Stripe, Salesforce, HubSpot
- OAuth flows
- Pre-built variable mappings
- Webhook support

### Phase 4: Streaming + Advanced (Week 7-8)
- Kafka/Kinesis consumer
- Real-time equation updates
- Data quality dashboard
- Schema drift detection

---

## 11. Summary

The Observation Fabric is not a RAG clone. It is a **structured data intelligence layer** that:
- Connects to any source (files, databases, APIs, tools, streams)
- Interprets schema semantics automatically
- Transforms raw data into validated observations
- Keeps equations alive with continuous sync
- Guarantees quality before data enters the reasoning pipeline

**The product promise**: *Drop your data. Ask quantitative questions. Get derived answers.*

The connector makes the Equation Layer accessible to non-technical users. Without it, the system is a powerful engine with no fuel. With it, the system becomes a self-improving quantitative brain for any business.

get_equation_graph(db_path) → _load_graph_from_library() (on first access)
  ↓
/v2/graph/* endpoints ← get_equation_graph()
```

### Categorical encoding
```
auto_encode(columns)
  → detect_categorical(vals)
    → strings → categorical
    → numeric, ≤2 unique → binary (label)
    → numeric, >2 unique → continuous (passthrough)
  → binary → label_encode
  → multi-cat → one_hot_encode(drop_first)
```

### Probabilistic pipeline
```
fit_canonical_families → store result.jac on EquationFit
  → solve() computes: fn(x_train), residuals = y - y_pred, residual_std
  → _hessian_from_jacobian(jac, residuals) → covariance
  → laplace_from_fit(params, cov, eq, x, residual_std) → CI + noise
```

## Real Questions EL Can Now Answer

| Question | How |
|----------|-----|
| "What drives churn?" | POST /v2/discover → variable ranking |
| "How sensitive is revenue to CAC?" | POST /v1/solve → elasticity |
| "What if pricing changes?" | POST /v2/what-if |
| "Which variable matters most?" | POST /v2/solve → importance |
| "What happens next quarter?" | POST /v2/forecast |
| "What drives NRR?" | POST /v2/decompose |
| "Why did profit change?" | POST /v2/explain → per-driver attribution |
| "Does X cause Y?" | POST /v2/causal/granger |
| "How does churn differ by segment?" | POST /v2/cohort/compare |
| "Can I chain price→demand→revenue?" | POST /v2/graph/path |

python -m pytest tests/ -q
```

Per the Architect's plan:

4	metadata:
5	  type: project
